annotations.name: managed
annotations.purpose: Suppress ownership and move-checking for the annotated item.

annotations.name: derive
annotations.purpose: Auto-derive trait implementations for a record or enum.

annotations.name: test
annotations.purpose: Mark a function as a unit test discovered by `bock test`.

annotations.name: requires
annotations.purpose: Declare the capabilities this function requires.

annotations.name: performance
annotations.purpose: Declare performance budgets enforced by the context pass.

strictness_levels.name: sketch
strictness_levels.description: Lenient mode: effects and capabilities are inferred; no diagnostics.

strictness_levels.name: development
strictness_levels.description: Public items must declare effects; private items are not checked.

strictness_levels.name: production
strictness_levels.description: Every function must declare its effects and capabilities.

codes.description: The lexer encountered a character it does not recognize as part of any valid token. (Lexer-only: the name-resolution pass reports an undefined name as `E1009`, an unfound module as `E1010`, and an unfound symbol as `E1011`.)
A string literal was opened but never closed before end of input.
An escape sequence in a string literal is not one of the recognized forms.
A character literal is empty, contains more than one character, or has an invalid escape.
A digit was found that is outside the range of the declared numeric base (e.g. `8` in an octal literal or a non-hex digit after `0x`).
A `/* ... */` block comment was opened but never closed before end of input.
An import was declared but never referenced. Uses of an imported effect name in `with`, `handling`, and `impl … for` positions count as references (Q-w1001-effect-import-false-positive).
The referenced symbol exists but is private; declare it `public` to export it.

The `use` import graph contains a cycle, so the modules cannot be compiled in any dependency order. The message names every module in the cycle in order and points at one offending `use` edge. Fix by removing one of the `use` edges in the cycle, or by extracting the shared items into a third module that both can import.

An identifier reference could not be resolved to any binding in scope. Most often a name is not imported or is misspelled; check the `use` declarations. (A bare effect operation such as `log` called outside a `with` clause or `handling` block is reported separately as `E6005`, not this code.) Emitted by the name-resolution pass.
A `use` path named a module the registry could not locate. Check the import path and that the target file declares `module <path>`. Emitted by the name-resolution pass.

codes.summary: Imported symbol not found in module.
codes.description: A `use` path names a module that exists but does not export the requested symbol. Emitted by the name-resolution pass.
The parser encountered unexpected input while reading a top-level item.
The next token did not match any alternative at the current grammar position.
The parser required a specific token here and found something else.
A top-level declaration has malformed structure.
An expression could not be parsed due to malformed structure.
A pattern in a `match` or `let` binding could not be parsed.
A type annotation could not be parsed as a valid type expression.

A construct that requires parentheses was written without them. Lambda parameters must be parenthesized; single-identifier forms (`x => …`) are not accepted. An `if` condition must be parenthesized (`if (cond) { … }`); the diagnostic names the offending token and a note gives the wrapped form. (A missing function name after `fn` is reported separately as `E2073`.)
A parameter position expected a name and found something else — e.g. a missing name after `mut`, or a non-identifier where a parameter name was required.
A generic parameter list is malformed.
A `use` import is malformed.
An `@annotation` or `#attribute` could not be parsed.
A `const` declaration was missing its name: a constant name was expected and a different token was found.
A match arm is malformed; each arm is `pattern => expression`.
An associated-type position inside a `trait`/`impl` expected a type name (`type Name`) and found a different token.
A method declaration inside a `trait`/`impl`/`class` expected a method name after `fn` and found a different token.
A top-level (or nested) function declaration expected a function name after `fn` and found a different token.
An `effect` declaration or `with` clause is malformed.
An effect operation declaration inside an `effect` block expected an operation name after `fn` and found a different token.

codes.summary: Tuple positional indexing is not available in v1.
codes.description: `t.0` / `t.1` positional tuple indexing is not a v1 form. Destructure with `let (a, b) = t` to bind tuple elements instead.

Expected and actual types do not unify. The message reads `expected `T`, found `U``: `T` is the type the surrounding context requires and `U` is the type the expression actually has, both in surface Bock syntax. When a direct conversion to the expected type exists, a note suggests it (`.to_float()`, `.to_int()`, `.to_string()`, or `Int.try_from`/`Float.try_from` for parsing a String).
A name referenced in an expression has no binding in scope.
The number of arguments does not match the callee's parameter count.
An expression of non-function type was used in a call position.
A refined-type predicate could not be satisfied.
Two `impl` blocks apply to the same type and violate coherence.

codes.summary: Cannot implement a core trait for a primitive type.
codes.description: Core traits (`Equatable`, `Comparable`, `Displayable`, `Hashable`) have sealed, compiler-provided conformances for primitive types. User code may not add its own `impl` of a core trait for a primitive (an orphan-rule violation). Wrap the primitive in a newtype and implement the trait for that instead.

A `.into()` call (or `from`/`try_from`) could not be resolved: no `From`/`Into`/`TryFrom` impl relates the source and target types. For `.into()` the target comes from the expected type, so the call site must have a reachable annotation (a `let y: U =`, an `fn -> U` return position, or a typed argument).

codes.summary: No such method on a concrete type.
codes.description: A method that does not exist on the receiver's concrete type was called. The receiver resolved to a fully-known type (a primitive, a built-in collection, an `Optional`/`Result`, or a user record/class/enum in scope) and the method is in none of that type's method sets (intrinsic, canonical-trait, inherent/trait impl, or inherited trait default). When a near-miss name exists a "did you mean `…`?" suggestion is offered. Not raised for unresolved inference variables or §4.9 sketch-mode receivers.

A `use` declaration named a module path with neither a brace-list nor a wildcard (a bare `use core.error`). Per §12.2 this is not a v1 import form; module-qualified access is deferred to v1.x. Import the names you need with the braced form (`use core.error.{ Error }`) or the discouraged wildcard (`use core.error.*`).

codes.summary: Operand or bound instantiation is not `Equatable`.
codes.description: An `==`/`!=` operand (or a type instantiating an `Equatable` bound) does not conform to `Equatable` (DQ29). Records and enums conform STRUCTURALLY iff every field / variant payload type conforms (recursively); `List[T]`/`Set[T]`/`Optional[T]` iff `T`, `Map[K, V]` iff `K` and `V`, `Result[T, E]` iff `T` and `E`, tuples iff all components; generic user types decide per instantiation. A non-Equatable leaf (e.g. an `Fn` field) poisons the type, and the message names the offending field path and type. Classes are excluded from the structural default and need an explicit `impl Equatable`. An explicit impl always wins over the structural rules. Fix: implement `Equatable` for the type, or remove the comparison.
A value was used after it had been moved into another binding or call.
The callee takes a `mut` borrow but the binding was not declared `mut`.
The loop body moves a value captured from outside, which would move it more than once.

An in-place `List` mutator (`push`/`append`, DQ18; `pop`/`remove_at`/`insert`/`reverse` and indexed `set`, DQ30) was called on a receiver that is not a mutable lvalue. These methods mutate the list in place, so the receiver must be a `mut` binding. Fix: declare the list with `let mut`, take a `mut` parameter, or use a value-returning combinator (`+` / `concat`).
A function uses an algebraic effect that is not in its declared `with` clause.

codes.summary: Public function has undeclared effect (development mode).
codes.description: A public function uses an effect that is not in its declared `with` clause. Promotes to error in production strictness.
A called function has an effect that the caller does not declare or handle.

codes.summary: Public function propagates undeclared effect (development mode).
codes.description: A public function calls a function whose effects escape its declared clause.

codes.summary: Effect operation called without its effect declared or handled.
codes.description: An effect operation (e.g. `log(...)` of `effect Log`) was called, but the operation's effect is neither declared by the enclosing function (`with <Effect>`) nor handled in an enclosing scope (a `handling (<Effect> with <handler>) { ... }` block or a module-level `handle <Effect> with <handler>`). Fix by declaring the effect on the function or installing a handler. Emitted by the name-resolution pass: effect operations are only in scope in those contexts.

codes.summary: Lambda-handler form is reserved until v1.x.
codes.description: The lambda-based handler surface `Effect.handler(op: (args) => ...)` is reserved for v1.x and is not a v1 form. v1 supports exactly one handler form: declare a record, `impl <Effect> for <Record>`, and install it with `handle <Effect> with <record>` (module level) or `handling (<Effect> with <record>) { ... }` (block level).
A function requires a capability that is not granted by the caller.

codes.summary: Public function requires uninstalled capability (development mode).
codes.description: A public function's required capability is not present in the caller's capability set.
A callee requires a capability the caller has not declared.
A public function calls into code requiring capabilities it has not declared.
A `@capability` annotation named a capability the compiler does not recognize.
A `@capability` argument was not a capability name (e.g. `Capability.Network` or `Network`).

codes.summary: Expected duration or byte size in `@performance`.
codes.description: A `@performance` budget argument was not a duration (e.g. `100.ms`) or a byte size (e.g. `50.mb`).
An `@invariant` annotation was written without the boolean expression it constrains.
A `@security` annotation expected a string `level` or a boolean `pii` flag, or was given neither.
A `@domain` annotation argument was not a string literal.
An `@invariant` expression must be a comparison, logical, or call expression that yields a boolean.

codes.summary: Child security level less restrictive than parent.
codes.description: An item's `@security` level is less restrictive than the level it inherits from an enclosing context, which would weaken the parent's guarantee.
PII status is inherited: a child cannot drop a parent's `pii=true` to `pii=false`. The narrower flag is ignored and a warning is issued.

codes.summary: Public item is missing context annotations (production mode).
codes.description: In production (strict) mode every public item (`fn`/`class`/`trait`/`record`/`enum`) must carry a `@context` annotation. The standard-mode form of this rule is the warning `W8013`.

codes.summary: Public item is missing context annotations (standard mode).
codes.description: In standard mode a public item (`fn`/`class`/`trait`/`record`/`enum`) without a `@context` annotation is flagged as a recommendation, once per item. Promotes to the error `E8013` in production (strict) mode; suppressed entirely in lax mode and for embedded-stdlib modules.
A `@security(level: …)` string was not one of the known security levels; the annotation is kept but flagged.
A `@performance` budget (`max_latency` or `max_memory`) was zero or negative; budgets must be positive values.

codes.summary: Effect operation has no handler in scope.
codes.description: A called effect operation has no handler installed in any enclosing scope and the effect is not declared, so it can never be discharged.

An effect declared in a function's `with` clause is never used in its body (capability verification). Drop the unused effect from the `with` clause. (A PII-tainted signature without a security context is reported separately as `W8023`.)
A called function requires a capability that is not declared in the current scope, so the requirement cannot be satisfied.

codes.summary: Importing a PII-returning function into a module without a PII security context.
codes.description: A `use` imports a function that returns PII-tainted types into a module whose `@security` context does not acknowledge PII.

codes.summary: PII-tainted type passed to a logging/output function.
codes.description: A PII-tainted type flows into a logging or output sink, which is a potential data leak. One warning is emitted per call site.

codes.summary: PII-tainted signature without a security context.
codes.description: A function has PII-tainted types in its signature but its module lacks a `@security` context acknowledging PII (e.g. `@security(level: "confidential")` or `@security(pii: true)`). Emitted by context composition.
In production mode a public function, class, trait, or type must carry a `@context` annotation; capability verification rejects the bare declaration.

commands.name: build
commands.summary: Transpile and compile a Bock project.

commands.name: run
commands.summary: Execute a Bock program via the interpreter.

commands.name: inspect
commands.summary: Browse AI decisions, rule cache, and AI response cache.

commands.name: pin
commands.summary: Pin AI decisions so they replay deterministically.

commands.name: unpin
commands.summary: Clear pin metadata from a decision.

commands.name: override
commands.summary: Override or promote an AI decision.

commands.name: cache
commands.summary: Manage on-disk AI, decision, and rule caches.

commands.name: promote
commands.summary: Analyze a project at the next strictness level.

commands.name: model
commands.summary: Query or interact with AI models.
